papers.title: Multi-omics analysis reveals mechanisms of Qingying granules in treating porcine warm disease: dosage optimization and systems biology insights.
papers.abstract: Warm diseases in swine, particularly those associated with highly pathogenic viruses such as African swine fever virus (ASFV) and porcine reproductive and respiratory syndrome virus (PRRSV), inflict devastating economic losses on global pork production. Current control strategies rely heavily on antibiotics and vaccines; however, emerging antimicrobial resistance and vaccine efficacy variability necessitate alternative therapeutic approaches. Traditional Chinese Veterinary Medicine offers potential solutions, yet rigorous evidence-based studies are required to meet modern regulatory standards.
papers.summary: This study uses multi-omics analysis to investigate how Qingying granules, a traditional Chinese veterinary medicine, treat warm disease in pigs caused by viruses like ASFV and PRRSV. The research identifies key mechanisms and optimal dosages, providing a systems biology perspective on this alternative therapeutic approach.

papers.title: Pathway-level proteomics identifies subcellular trafficking dysregulation as a molecular signature in severe traumatic brain injury.
papers.abstract: Traumatic brain injury (TBI) remains a leading cause of death and disability worldwide in intensive care units, yet no targeted neuroprotective therapies exist despite over 300 clinical trials. This therapeutic failure stems partly from biological heterogeneity and the limitations of single-biomarker approaches that cannot capture the multifaceted pathophysiology of secondary brain injury. Systems biology approaches examining dysregulated pathways rather than isolated proteins may reveal candidate endotypes suitable for patient stratification and identify potential therapeutic targets.
papers.journal: Inflammation research : official journal of the European Histamine Research Society ... [et al.]
papers.summary: This study used a systems biology approach to analyze proteomic data from severe traumatic brain injury patients, finding that dysregulation of subcellular trafficking pathways is a key molecular signature. By focusing on disrupted pathways rather than individual proteins, the authors identified potential patient endotypes and therapeutic targets that could help explain why previous single-biomarker clinical trials have failed.

papers.title: MondrianMap: Navigating Gene Set Hierarchies with Multi-Resolution Enrichment Maps.
papers.abstract: Gene Ontology encodes genes as a hierarchy, yet every enrichment visualization flattens it into a ranked list, discarding the ability to view the same process at different levels of abstraction. We present MondrianMap, a free interactive web application ( https://mondrianmap.smartdrugdiscovery.org/ ) that organizes enrichment results into 13 semantically principled layers derived from the GOALS framework and renders them as color-encoded rectangular maps where block area reflects significance, color encodes effect direction, and spatial proximity preserves semantic relations, all within an int
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: MondrianMap is a free interactive web tool that visualizes Gene Ontology enrichment results as multi-resolution rectangular maps, preserving the hierarchical structure of gene sets rather than flattening them into ranked lists. It organizes results into 13 semantic layers, using block area for significance, color for effect direction, and spatial proximity for semantic relations.

papers.title: The genetic signature of memory encoding along the human hippocampal axis.
papers.abstract: Episodic memory formation engages hippocampal oscillations that vary along the anterior-posterior axis, but the molecular programs supporting this physiological specialization remain unclear. Here, we leveraged a rare neurosurgical dataset in which patients performed verbal episodic memory tasks during intrahippocampal intracranial EEG recordings prior to en bloc hippocampal resection, enabling integration of encoding-related oscillatory signatures with matched cell-type-resolved transcriptomics from the same individuals. Subsequent memory effects (SMEs) spanned delta/theta, gamma, and hippoca
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study links specific brain oscillations during memory encoding to gene expression patterns along the human hippocampus. By combining intracranial EEG recordings with transcriptomic data from the same patients, the researchers identified distinct molecular programs associated with memory-related neural activity.

papers.title: From plasmid sequence to process design: A computational analysis of metabolism in the context of plasmid DNA manufacturing.
papers.abstract: Detailed understanding of plasmid-host physiological interactions and recombinant protein expression is crucial for the design and optimization of plasmid DNA (pDNA) manufacturing processes. Successfully transformed host cells carrying one or more copies of a particular plasmid exhibit modified metabolic behavior which may include reduced specific growth rate, increased metabolic resource cycling (ATP/ADP, NAD/NADH, NADP/NADPH) and, in some cases, even modified nutrient uptake and metabolite secretion rates. Mapping plasmid design features to critical process parameters (CPPs), such as specifi
papers.summary: This study uses computational modeling to analyze how plasmid DNA (pDNA) affects host cell metabolism during manufacturing, showing that transformed cells exhibit reduced growth, increased energy cycling, and altered nutrient uptake. By mapping plasmid design features to critical process parameters, the work provides a framework for optimizing pDNA production.

papers.title: SimMapNet: a Bayesian framework for gene regulatory network inference using gene ontology similarities as external hint.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory network (GRN) reconstruction is a fundamental challenge in computational biology, and is crucial for understanding gene interactions. In this study, we aim to incorporate Gene Ontology (GO) similarities into the construction of GRNs. Our key assumption is that genes with higher similarity in Molecular Function, Biological Process, or Cellular Component categories are more likely to be functionally related and, therefore, more likely to be connected in the network. We introduce SimMapNet, a Bayesian framework that estimates the precision matrix, which serves as the adjacency mat
papers.summary: SimMapNet is a new Bayesian method that uses Gene Ontology (GO) similarity scores as prior information to improve the inference of gene regulatory networks. The approach assumes that genes sharing similar GO annotations are more likely to be functionally connected, and it estimates a precision matrix to represent network edges. This work provides a way to integrate functional knowledge into network reconstruction, potentially yielding more biologically meaningful regulatory predictions.

papers.title: D-SPIN constructs regulatory network models from scRNA-seq that reveal organizing principles of perturbation response.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory networks modulate the expression of the genome in response to signals and environmental conditions. Reconstructions of such networks can reveal the control principles cells use to maintain homeostasis and execute cell-state transitions. Here, we introduce a computational framework, dimension-scalable single-cell perturbation integration network (D-SPIN), that infers mechanistically interpretable and generative models of gene regulatory networks from single-cell mRNA-seq datasets collected across thousands of perturbation conditions. The models explain how perturbations modulate
papers.summary: D-SPIN is a new computational framework that builds interpretable gene regulatory network models from single-cell RNA-seq data collected across thousands of perturbation conditions. It reveals how cells organize their responses to perturbations, providing insights into the control principles underlying homeostasis and cell-state transitions.

papers.title: The mRNA covalent modification dihydrouridine regulates transcript turnover and photosynthetic capacity during plant abiotic stress.
papers.abstract: RNA covalent modifications (RCMs) influence RNA stability and translation efficiency, and thus play critical roles in eukaryotic growth and development. However, their role in regulating plant performance under abiotic stress remains largely unexplored. Here, we integrated multi-omics data in six Sorghum bicolor accessions under water-limiting conditions in the field to explore the relationship between RCMs and drought response. Within a stress and photosynthesis-associated gene co-expression module, we identified SbDUS2, a member of a family of enzymes conserved across eukaryotes, that cataly
papers.summary: This study shows that the RNA modification dihydrouridine, catalyzed by the enzyme SbDUS2, regulates transcript turnover and photosynthetic capacity in sorghum under drought stress. By integrating multi-omics data, the authors demonstrate that this covalent modification plays a key role in plant abiotic stress response, revealing a new layer of gene regulation.

papers.title: Deep dynamical models of single-cell multiomic velocities predict loss-of-function and rescue perturbations in B cells.
papers.abstract: We present DynaVelo, a generative neural ordinary differential equation model that learns the joint dynamics of gene expression and transcription factor (TF) motif activities in evolving cell systems using single-cell multiome with joint gene expression and chromatin accesibility readout. DynaVelo leverages partial RNA velocity information together with single-cell TF motif accessibility data to improve the modeling of cell state dynamics and identification of TF drivers. We show that DynaVelo recovers the complex and bifurcating in vivo dynamics of wildtype murine germinal center (GC) B cells
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: DynaVelo is a new computational model that uses single-cell multiomic data to jointly learn the dynamics of gene expression and transcription factor activity. It improves the modeling of cell state transitions and identifies key transcription factor drivers, successfully recovering the complex dynamics of B cell development in mice.

papers.title: pyVIPER: a fast and scalable Python package for protein activity estimation and master regulator analysis of single-cell RNA sequencing data.
papers.abstract: Single-cell sequencing has revolutionized biomedical research by offering insights into cellular heterogeneity at unprecedented resolution. Yet, the low signal-to-noise ratio characteristic of single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) challenges quantitative analyses. Gene regulatory network (GRN) analysis can help overcome this obstacle, enabling the mechanistic elucidation of cellular state determinants. For instance, the VIPER algorithm can identify Master Regulator proteins from gene expression data. However, as the size and complexity of scRNA-seq datasets grow, the demand for scalable tools
papers.summary: pyVIPER is a new Python package that efficiently estimates protein activity and identifies master regulator proteins from single-cell RNA sequencing data. It scales to large datasets, addressing the computational bottleneck of applying the VIPER algorithm to modern scRNA-seq experiments.

papers.title: A mechanistic computational model of the HIF signaling pathway in endothelial cells.
papers.abstract: In conditions such as cancer, cardiovascular diseases, and retinal diseases, cells under hypoxia activate oxygen-sensing mechanisms, promoting adaptation and survival. Many hypoxia computational models predate standardized identifiability analyses and lack systematic treatment of the HIF isoform-specific dynamics in endothelial cells. We present a technically validated mechanistic model of the HIF pathway in endothelial cells, capturing graded oxygen sensitivity and the transition from HIF1α-dominated acute to HIF2α-dominated prolonged hypoxic responses. Following identifiability analyses, the
papers.summary: This paper presents a new computational model of the HIF signaling pathway specifically in endothelial cells, which captures how cells sense and respond to different oxygen levels over time. The model is unique because it systematically accounts for the shift from HIF1α-driven acute responses to HIF2α-driven prolonged responses, and it includes rigorous validation through identifiability analyses. This work provides a more accurate tool for studying hypoxia-related diseases like cancer and cardiovascular disorders.

papers.title: Gastric cancer-secreted galectin-1 promotes peritoneal mesothelial-mesenchymal transition to prime peritoneal metastasis soil.
papers.abstract: Peritoneal metastasis (PM) is the most common cause of death in gastric cancer (GC) patients. Targeting the molecular mechanisms of PM in GC could reduce its incidence and improve clinical outcomes. Using clinical specimen analysis, lentivirus transfection, western blotting, RT-qPCR, immunofluorescence, cell adhesion assays, and a GC peritoneal metastasis (GCPM) animal model, we explored galectin-1's role in promoting peritoneal MMT (mesothelial-mesenchymal transition) and enhancing PM through in vivo and in vitro studies. Galectin-1 in the GC microenvironment reprograms peritoneal mesothelial
papers.summary: This study identifies galectin-1 secreted by gastric cancer cells as a key factor that reprograms peritoneal mesothelial cells through mesothelial-mesenchymal transition, thereby creating a favorable environment for peritoneal metastasis. The findings suggest that targeting galectin-1 could be a potential therapeutic strategy to reduce peritoneal metastasis in gastric cancer patients.

papers.title: Cold induced pain elicits reproducible breath metabolomic responses across geographically distinct populations.
papers.abstract: Observer-independent assessment of nociceptive states is particularly important in non-communicative or vulnerable patient populations, where self-report is unavailable. In this proof-of-concept study, we explored whether exhaled breath metabolomics, measured in real time using secondary electrospray ionization high-resolution mass spectrometry (SESI-HRMS), can capture rapid physiological responses to the cold pressor test (CPT), a standardized model of acute sympathetic and nociceptive activation. Forty healthy adults (n = 19 in Switzerland, n = 21 in China) underwent four breath measurements
papers.summary: This proof-of-concept study shows that real-time analysis of exhaled breath metabolites using mass spectrometry can detect rapid physiological responses to cold-induced pain. The breath metabolomic responses were reproducible across two geographically distinct populations in Switzerland and China, suggesting this non-invasive approach could potentially be used to assess pain in patients who cannot communicate.

papers.title: Protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data combining unsupervised and supervised data integrating approaches.
papers.abstract: Multi-omics integration combines data from transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics to provide insights into biological systems. Here, we present a protocol for integrating and interpreting multi-omics data using unsupervised multi-omics factor analysis (MOFA), supervised projection-based integration (data integration analysis for biomarker discovery using latent components, DIABLO), along with general single-omics analysis techniques and visualizations. We describe steps for data preparation, model construction, and biological interpretation of multi-omics datasets. These approaches iden
papers.summary: This protocol describes a step-by-step approach for integrating transcriptomics, proteomics, and metabolomics data using both unsupervised (MOFA) and supervised (DIABLO) methods. It covers data preparation, model building, and biological interpretation, providing a practical guide for multi-omics analysis.

papers.title: A causal reinforcement learning framework for reliable gene regulatory network inference.
papers.abstract: Gene regulatory network inference is crucial for revealing the mechanisms of cellular functions and the causal mechanisms of disease occurrence. However, traditional correlation-based methods struggle to identify causal directions, and deep learning methods lack structural interpretability. Moreover, neither of them can efficiently adapt to the search of the solution space for large-scale gene regulatory networks, making it difficult to accurately capture the real regulatory patterns between genes. This study proposes a gene regulatory network inference method based on causal reinforcement lea
papers.summary: This paper introduces a new method for inferring gene regulatory networks that combines causal inference with reinforcement learning, overcoming limitations of traditional correlation-based and deep learning approaches. The method improves the ability to identify causal directions between genes and scales better to large networks, leading to more accurate reconstruction of regulatory relationships.

papers.title: WNT signaling in human pluripotent stem cells promotes HDAC2-dependent epigenetic programs and development of retinoic acid-responsive mesoderm.
papers.abstract: Human pluripotent stem cells (hPSCs) can be used as a scalable source of lymphocytes for adoptive cell therapies, contingent on the robust generation of definitive hematopoietic intermediates. Early WNT activation with CHIR99021 during mesoderm induction promoted the formation of KDR+ ALDH1A2+ mesoderm, which is essential for subsequent generation of T cells in a retinoic acid (RA)-dependent manner. Integrated scRNA-seq and ATAC-seq defined a WNT-dependent developmental trajectory from hPSCs to KDR+ ALDH1A2+ mesoderm. Gene regulatory network modeling predicted HDAC2 and E-box transcription fac
papers.summary: This study shows that early WNT signaling activation in human pluripotent stem cells drives the formation of a specific mesoderm population (KDR+ ALDH1A2+) that is critical for generating T cells. Using single-cell RNA and ATAC sequencing, the authors mapped the developmental trajectory and identified HDAC2 as a key epigenetic regulator of this process. The findings provide a roadmap for improving the production of T cells for adoptive cell therapies.

papers.title: Remodelling of miRNA Regulatory Landscape During West Nile Virus (WNV) Infection.
papers.abstract: Background/Objectives: West Nile virus (WNV) remains a significant threat to human health, with no approved antiviral treatments or vaccine available. A better understanding of the molecular mechanisms governing flavivirus-host interactions is needed to identify host regulatory pathways involved in infection. This study aimed to investigate how WNV infection remodels the host miRNA-mRNA regulatory landscape. Methods: WNV-induced changes in host miRNA expression in HEK-293 cells were profiled using miRNA-Seq. Transcriptome-wide host gene expression changes in WNV-infected cells were analysed us
papers.summary: This study investigates how West Nile virus (WNV) infection alters the host's miRNA-mRNA regulatory landscape, using miRNA-Seq and transcriptome profiling in HEK-293 cells. The findings reveal specific miRNA expression changes that may influence host pathways during infection, offering insights into potential targets for antiviral strategies.

papers.title: Mitochondrial Complex I Modulator Restores Network Resilience in Advanced Alzheimer's Disease Through Metabolic Reprogramming.
papers.abstract: Mitochondrial dysfunction and lipid dysregulation are among the earliest abnormalities in Alzheimer's disease (AD), yet their mechanistic interplay and therapeutic potential remain poorly understood. Here, we investigated whether restoration of mitochondrial function can reverse metabolic dysfunction and promote resilience in advanced-stage AD. Female APP/PS1 mice were treated with the brain-penetrant mitochondrial complex I (mtCI) modulator CP2 beginning at 19 months of age, when pathology and cognitive deficits were well established. To define the metabolic mechanisms underlying therapeutic
papers.journal: bioRxiv : the preprint server for biology
papers.summary: This study shows that treating aged Alzheimer's model mice with a drug that modulates mitochondrial complex I can reverse metabolic dysfunction and restore brain network resilience even at advanced disease stages. The findings suggest that targeting mitochondrial metabolism may be a viable therapeutic strategy for late-stage Alzheimer's disease.

papers.title: A community reconstruction of Chinese hamster metabolism and structural systems biology elucidate metabolic rewiring in lactate-free CHO cells.
papers.abstract: Genome-scale metabolic models (GEMs) are indispensable for studying cellular metabolism. We present iCHO3K, a community-consensus, manually curated reconstruction of the Chinese hamster metabolic network. Spanning 11,004 reactions linked to 3,597 genes, iCHO3K augments the network with 3,489 protein structures and physicochemical descriptors for >70% of 7,377 metabolites, enabling structure-aware analyses. We applied iCHO3K to contextualize transcriptomics and metabolomics from a fed-batch Chinese hamster ovary (CHO) cell line engineered to abolish lactate secretion. The model indicated reduce
papers.summary: Researchers built iCHO3K, a comprehensive genome-scale metabolic model of Chinese hamster cells that includes protein structures for most enzymes. Using this model, they analyzed engineered CHO cells that don't secrete lactate and found that the cells rewire their metabolism to maintain energy production without producing this waste product.

papers.title: IAN, an intelligent system for omics data analysis and discovery.
papers.abstract: IAN is an R package addressing the challenge of integrating, analyzing, and interpreting gene expression data using an artificial intelligence (AI) system. It leverages popular pathway (KEGG, WikiPathways, Reactome, and GO) and regulatory datasets (ChEA), along with STRING for protein-protein interactions, to perform standard enrichment analysis. A multi-agent architecture uses individual enrichment results to generate summaries using large language models (LLMs). These summaries are contextually integrated and interpreted by the LLM, guided by engineered prompts and grounding instructions, to
papers.summary: IAN is an R package that uses a multi-agent AI system to integrate gene expression data with pathway and regulatory databases, then employs large language models to generate contextual summaries of enrichment results. This approach simplifies the interpretation of complex omics data by producing human-readable insights from standard bioinformatics analyses.

papers.title: Integrated multi-dimensional modeling of non-model bacteria identifies engineering targets for acarbose biosynthesis optimization.
papers.abstract: Metabolic engineering for high-value compounds such as acarbose, a diabetes drug, requires systematic understanding of metabolic regulation. Here, we applied a multi-dimensional systems biology approach in Actinoplanes sp. SE50/110, a non-model acarbose-producing bacterium. We reconstructed an improved genome-scale metabolic model (iASE1267) with expanded metabolic coverage and a MEMOTE score of 80%, enabling more accurate phenotype predictions. Using a dual-objective OptRAM strain design strategy, we identified two sets of static engineering targets, including AcbR overexpression with adenylo
papers.summary: This study developed an improved genome-scale metabolic model for the non-model bacterium Actinoplanes sp. SE50/110, enabling more accurate predictions for engineering acarbose production. Using a dual-objective computational strategy, the authors identified specific genetic targets, such as AcbR overexpression, that can optimize biosynthesis of this diabetes drug.

papers.title: Hybrid genome‑scale metabolic modeling and machine learning reveal cost‑efficient strategies for phototrophic polyhydroxybutyrate production in Rhodopseudomonas palustris.
papers.abstract: Plastic pollution from fossil-based materials is a major global environmental challenge. Microbially derived bioplastics, such as polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB), offer a promising biodegradable alternative. However, the high substrate and operational costs of PHB production remain a major barrier to large-scale deployment. Optimizing PHB synthesis requires navigating a multidimensional design space of metabolic, nutritional, and operational variables, which is impractical to explore experimentally. This study presents an integrated computational framework that combines a genome-scale metabolic mode
papers.summary: This study combines a genome-scale metabolic model with machine learning to identify cost-effective strategies for producing the bioplastic polyhydroxybutyrate (PHB) in the bacterium Rhodopseudomonas palustris. The computational framework efficiently explores the complex design space of metabolic and operational variables, revealing optimal conditions that could lower production costs and support large-scale bioplastic manufacturing.

papers.title: Systematic design of auxotrophic strains and media conditions to probe metabolic functions in E. coli.
papers.abstract: Despite progress in automated gene annotation, many deficiencies and knowledge gaps remain, even for well-studied organisms. Of particular concern is the accuracy and detail of annotations for transporters of various organic substrates and products of metabolism and for enzymes that do not share sequence homology with well-characterized strains. Unfortunately, annotation errors present in earlier genome-scale metabolic (GSM) models propagate to newer models with few opportunities for later correction. Here, we introduce a systematic computational procedure that applies the Escherichia coli gen
papers.summary: This study presents a computational method to systematically design auxotrophic E. coli strains and corresponding media conditions, enabling targeted probing of metabolic functions. The approach helps identify and correct annotation errors in genome-scale metabolic models, particularly for transporters and non-homologous enzymes, by testing growth requirements.

papers.title: Tensor network-based gene regulatory network inference for single-cell transcriptomic data.
papers.abstract: Deciphering complex gene-gene interactions remains challenging in transcriptomics as traditional methods often miss higher-order and nonlinear dependencies. This study introduces a quantum-inspired framework leveraging tensor networks to optimally map expression data into a lower dimensional representation preserving biological locality. Using quantum mutual information (QMI), a nonparametric measure natural for tensor networks, we quantify gene dependencies and establish statistical significance via permutation testing. From those values, we construct optimal network where genes are positione
papers.summary: This paper introduces a quantum-inspired tensor network framework for inferring gene regulatory networks from single-cell transcriptomic data. The method uses quantum mutual information to capture higher-order and nonlinear gene dependencies, preserving biological locality in a lower-dimensional representation. It offers a novel approach to reconstructing gene networks that traditional methods may miss.

papers.title: Matched sampling reveals uncoupled phenotypic plasticity during metastasis formation in uveal melanoma.
papers.abstract: Uveal melanoma (UM) is a genetically well-defined yet phenotypically diverse malignancy with a strong predilection for liver metastasis. To define phenotypic divergence during progression, we performed comprehensive gene expression profiling of primary UM (pUM) and metastatic UM (mUM) samples. Two principal molecular subtypes emerged in both entities. Matched pUM-mUM samples showed little transcriptional concordance, indicating extensive reprogramming during metastasis. Approximately half of the pUM samples exhibited a proliferative, MAPK/PI3K-driven phenotype, suggesting heterogeneous depende
papers.summary: This study shows that uveal melanoma (UM) undergoes extensive transcriptional reprogramming during metastasis, with little concordance between matched primary and metastatic samples. Two principal molecular subtypes were found in both primary and metastatic UM, and about half of primary tumors had a proliferative, MAPK/PI3K-driven phenotype, indicating heterogeneous dependencies.

papers.title: A metabolic model of the leaf-associated bacterium Paraburkholderia dioscoreae links the ethylene precursor ACC to core carbon pathways.
papers.abstract: Paraburkholderia dioscoreae MSB3T is a novel species with potential agricultural impact, isolated from leaf acumens of the "air potato yam" Dioscorea bulbifera. P. dioscoreae's genome encodes a relevant combination of features mediating a beneficial plant-associated lifestyle, and it exhibits significant growth promotion when applied to agriculturally important plants such as tomato. Here, we constructed iPR1691, the first genome-scale metabolic model for P. dioscoreae MSB3, which includes 1,687 reactions, 1,487 metabolites, and 1,691 genes. From an available annotation of P. dioscoreae's geno
papers.summary: Researchers built the first genome-scale metabolic model for the plant-beneficial bacterium Paraburkholderia dioscoreae, linking the ethylene precursor ACC to core carbon pathways. The model reveals how this bacterium may promote plant growth, offering a tool to understand and potentially enhance its agricultural applications.

papers.title: Decoding the epithelial-stromal interactome in allergic rhinitis through single-cell multi-omics integration.
papers.abstract: Allergic rhinitis (AR) is a prevalent chronic condition, yet the cellular and molecular changes associated with its pathogenesis remain incompletely understood.
papers.journal: The Journal of allergy and clinical immunology
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell multi-omics to map interactions between epithelial and stromal cells in allergic rhinitis, revealing key cellular and molecular changes that drive the condition. The findings provide a detailed view of the disease's pathogenesis, offering potential targets for therapy.

papers.title: Aerobic conditioning and ginsenoside modulation modify molecular resilience in middle-aged rats: Differential effects of individual and combined interventions.
papers.abstract: Hippocampal aging is characterized by early cognitive decline, disrupted redox homeostasis, and shifts in amyloid processing. This study evaluated the modulatory effects of aerobic exercise and ginseng supplementation, both individually and concurrently, against these alterations. Middle-aged Wistar rats (n = 30) were allocated into young control, middle-aged control, exercise, ginseng supplementation, and combined intervention groups, and assessed through T-maze and Morris Water Maze tasks. Hippocampal integrity was examined with Cresyl violet staining and Nrf2 protein immunohistochemistry. G
papers.summary: This study in middle-aged rats found that aerobic exercise and ginseng supplementation, alone or combined, can counteract age-related cognitive decline and hippocampal changes by modulating redox homeostasis and amyloid processing. The combined intervention showed differential effects compared to individual treatments, suggesting potential for multi-modal strategies to promote brain resilience during aging.

papers.title: Exact Discrete Stochastic Simulation With Deep-Learning-Scale Gradient Optimization.
papers.abstract: Exact stochastic simulation of continuous-time Markov chains (CTMCs) is essential when discreteness and noise drive system behavior, but the hard categorical event selection in Gillespie-type algorithms blocks gradient-based learning. We eliminate this constraint by decoupling forward simulation from backward differentiation, with hard categorical sampling generating exact trajectories and gradients propagating through a continuous massively-parallel Gumbel-Softmax straight-through surrogate. Our approach enables accurate optimization at parameter scales over four orders of magnitude beyond ex
papers.summary: This paper introduces a method that allows gradient-based optimization to be applied to exact stochastic simulations of continuous-time Markov chains (like Gillespie algorithms), which was previously impossible due to the discrete nature of event selection. By using a Gumbel-Softmax straight-through estimator to decouple forward simulation from backward differentiation, the approach enables accurate optimization across parameter scales spanning over four orders of magnitude. This breakthrough opens the door for deep learning to optimize parameters in systems where randomness and discrete events are critical, such as in biochemical reaction networks.

papers.title: A metabolic model based on a pangenome core reveals putative conserved biochemical features of the phytopathogen Xylella fastidiosa.
papers.abstract: Xylella fastidiosa is a xylem-limited phytopathogenic bacterium responsible for severe diseases in many economically important crops. Despite its impact, its metabolism remains poorly characterized due to fastidious growth and the limited availability of defined culture media. Here, we reconstruct the first pangenome-based genome-scale metabolic model for X. fastidiosa, integrating conserved metabolic functions from 18 strains across five subspecies. The resulting consensus model, iXfcore, is manually curated and used to explore the species' metabolic capabilities. Model simulations predict mi
papers.summary: Researchers built the first pangenome-based metabolic model for the plant pathogen Xylella fastidiosa, integrating data from 18 strains to identify core metabolic functions. This model, iXfcore, helps predict the bacterium's metabolic capabilities, which were previously poorly understood due to its difficult growth conditions.

papers.title: Single-cell and spatial omics in plants: from cellular atlases to regulatory mechanisms.
papers.abstract: Single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) has transformed transcriptomic studies by enabling gene expression profiling at the resolution of individual cells within and across a broad range of tissue types, revealing cellular heterogeneity that is obscured in bulk tissue transcriptomes. Over the past decade, improvements in microfluidics and library preparation have drastically increased throughput, allowing tens of thousands of cells to be assayed in a single experiment. Although initially developed in animal systems, scRNA-seq has rapidly emerged as a powerful and widely adopted approach in plan
papers.summary: This review discusses how single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq) and spatial omics technologies are being applied to plants, enabling researchers to build cellular atlases and uncover regulatory mechanisms at unprecedented resolution. The authors highlight recent advances in microfluidics and library preparation that have made these methods widely accessible for plant biology.

papers.title: Causal intervention validation of gene regulatory signals in scGPT.
papers.abstract: Single-cell foundation models such as scGPT have been promoted as representations of gene regulation, but their advertised regulatory signal has been judged largely from attention weights, which are correlational. We ask a deliberately bounded question: whether direct interventions on scGPT gene tokens-causal with respect to the model's own computation-recover model-internal transcription-factor (TF)-target dependencies that align with curated references, whether that signal is robust, and whether it transfers to real perturbation responses. We separate these into two explicit validation axes
papers.summary: This study tests whether the single-cell foundation model scGPT captures genuine regulatory signals by performing causal interventions on its gene tokens, rather than relying on correlational attention weights. The authors find that model-internal transcription factor-target dependencies can be recovered through these interventions, and they assess the robustness and transferability of this signal to real perturbation responses.

papers.title: Thermo-flux: generation and analysis of thermodynamic-stoichiometric metabolic network models.
papers.abstract: Metabolic modeling with stoichiometric models and flux balance analysis (FBA) has greatly advanced our understanding of metabolism. However, valid FBA predictions require mechanistically correct constraints. Thermodynamic constraints can increase the mechanistic foundations of stoichiometric models and reduce the solution space, but incorporating them has so far required cumbersome manual effort. To circumvent manual curation, we introduce 'Thermo-Flux', a semi-automated Python package that converts stoichiometric models into comprehensive thermodynamic-stoichiometric models. 'Thermo-Flux' ena
papers.summary: Thermo-Flux is a new Python package that semi-automatically adds thermodynamic constraints to standard metabolic models, reducing the need for manual curation. This makes it easier to generate more realistic flux balance analysis predictions by narrowing the possible solution space. The tool should help researchers build more mechanistically accurate models of metabolism.

papers.title: SOPA and SIMPA: normalized single-sample integrated multiomics pathway analysis of tumor heterogeneity in solid cancers.
papers.abstract: Inter-sample tumor heterogeneity poses significant challenges to metastatic cancer treatment. Although multiomics analyses provide nuanced molecular insights into tumor heterogeneity, current molecular pathway analysis tools focus on group-based comparisons, which may overlook differential single-sample perturbations. Here, we present normalized single-sample single-omic pathway analysis (SOPA), and its extension, normalized single-sample integrated multiomics pathway analysis (SIMPA), as a bioinformatics pipeline for performing supervised differential pathway analysis. The pipeline utilizes c
papers.summary: This paper introduces SOPA and SIMPA, two new bioinformatics tools for performing single-sample pathway analysis using multiomics data. These methods enable researchers to assess pathway perturbations in individual tumor samples, which is particularly useful for understanding tumor heterogeneity in metastatic cancers.

papers.title: Integrating WGCNA and machine learning to identify and validate key biomarkers in MASLD.
papers.abstract: Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD) represents the most prevalent chronic liver disease worldwide. The absence of approved pharmacotherapies is largely attributed to their profound molecular heterogeneity. The identification of novel hub genes in MASLD is therefore critical for unraveling the complex molecular mechanisms driving disease pathogenesis and progression.
papers.summary: This study combines WGCNA and machine learning to identify key biomarkers for Metabolic Dysfunction-Associated Steatotic Liver Disease (MASLD), a common chronic liver condition. The findings highlight novel hub genes that could help explain the molecular heterogeneity of MASLD and potentially guide future therapeutic development.

papers.title: NSAIDs trigger drug-specific response to exercise: a multi-omics systems biology analysis of a randomized crossover trial.
papers.abstract: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (NSAIDs) are widely used by athletes and those who exercise, yet their influence on the molecular responses to exercise remains unclear. Prior studies have often focused on a limited set of molecular pathways, potentially overlooking broader regulator effects of NSAIDs on skeletal muscle signaling. Therefore, we conducted a systems biology study of skeletal muscle biopsies taken before and after exercise, in combination with NSAID consumption, using transcriptomics and metabolomics, to identify differentially enriched pathways and biofunctions.
papers.summary: This study used a multi-omics approach (transcriptomics and metabolomics) to investigate how NSAIDs alter the molecular response to exercise in skeletal muscle. By analyzing biopsies from a randomized crossover trial, they found that NSAIDs trigger drug-specific changes in gene expression and metabolic pathways, rather than simply blocking inflammation. The findings suggest that NSAID use during exercise has broader, previously unrecognized effects on muscle signaling.

papers.title: Metabolic Task Analysis Reveals Distinct Metabotypes in End-Stage Dilated Cardiomyopathy.
papers.abstract: Dilated cardiomyopathy (DCM) is associated with shifts in cardiac metabolism. However, those shifts vary widely across patients, likely reflecting the diverse underlying causes of the disease. Identifying metabolic subtypes, or metabotypes, in DCM patients could help tailor treatments to patient needs. Hence, having a practical approach to identify these metabotypes would be a significant advance toward precision medicine in DCM.
papers.summary: This study identifies distinct metabolic subtypes (metabotypes) in end-stage dilated cardiomyopathy patients, revealing that metabolic shifts vary widely due to diverse underlying causes. The findings provide a practical approach for classifying these metabotypes, which could enable more personalized treatments and advance precision medicine for DCM.

papers.title: MERLIN-SUITE: Probabilistic modular GRN inference from multi-omics data integrating regulatory priors and transcription factor activity.
papers.abstract: Accurately reconstructing gene regulatory networks (GRNs) is essential for understanding transcriptional processes in development and disease. MERLIN-SUITE (https://github.com/Roy-lab/MERLIN-SUITE) represents a collection of algorithmic extensions based on MERLIN (Modular regulatory network learning with per gene information) a probabilistic framework that infers gene-specific and module-specific regulatory programs of co-regulated modules, capturing both detailed and modular aspects of transcriptional networks. While expression-based inference is effective, it often aligns poorly with experim
papers.summary: MERLIN-SUITE is a new computational framework that improves gene regulatory network inference by integrating multi-omics data (like ATAC-seq and ChIP-seq) with transcription factor activity, overcoming the limitations of expression-only approaches. It extends the existing MERLIN algorithm to incorporate regulatory priors and modular network learning, providing more accurate and biologically meaningful reconstructions of transcriptional programs.

papers.title: Spatial Transcriptomics in Ovarian Biology Technologies, Computational Challenges, and Biological Insights.
papers.abstract: The ovary is a structurally complex organ whose function depends on precisely coordinated interactions among multiple cell types. Spatially resolved transcriptomics (ST) has emerged as a powerful complement to single-cell RNA sequencing (scRNA-seq), enabling gene expression profiling within intact tissue and preserving the spatial context that dissociation-based methods inherently lack. This review provides a comprehensive overview of the major ST platforms, including sequencing-based technologies (Visium, Visium HD, Stereo-seq, and GeoMx) and imaging-based technologies (Xenium, MERSCOPE, and
papers.summary: This review discusses how spatially resolved transcriptomics (ST) technologies, such as Visium and Xenium, are being applied to study the complex cellular architecture of the ovary, overcoming limitations of single-cell RNA sequencing by preserving spatial context. It covers major ST platforms and their computational challenges, highlighting the biological insights gained into ovarian function and disease.

papers.title: Single-cell multi-omic atlas and morphogen screening informs midbrain and hindbrain organoid engineering.
papers.abstract: Patterning of the neural tube establishes midbrain and hindbrain structures that coordinate motor movement, process sensory input and integrate cognitive functions. Cellular impairment within these structures underlies diverse neurological disorders, and in vitro organoid models promise inroads to understanding development and modeling disease. Here, we use paired single-cell transcriptome and accessible chromatin sequencing to map cell composition and regulatory mechanisms in organoid models of midbrain and hindbrain. We find that existing midbrain organoid protocols generate ventral and dors
papers.summary: This study uses single-cell multi-omic sequencing to map cell types and regulatory mechanisms in midbrain and hindbrain organoids, revealing that current protocols produce ventral and dorsal cell types. The findings provide a resource for improving organoid engineering to better model neurological disorders.

papers.title: Ensemble threshold Boolean modeling reveals robust attractors and regulatory drivers in pediatric leukemia.
papers.abstract: Pediatric B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia (B-ALL) is characterized by substantial phenotypic heterogeneity, reflecting the complex structure of its underlying gene regulatory network (GRN). To investigate the dynamical principles governing these phenotypes, we model a curated GRN as a threshold Boolean network (TBN) and analyze its behavior across a large ensemble of weighted network realizations sharing the same topology. For each realization, we compute all asymptotic states and project them onto leukemia-relevant key genes to obtain a reduced attractor representation. The ensemble revea
papers.summary: This study models the gene regulatory network of pediatric B-cell acute lymphoblastic leukemia using an ensemble of threshold Boolean networks, revealing robust attractor states that correspond to known leukemia phenotypes. The approach identifies key regulatory drivers of these stable states, offering insights into the dynamical principles underlying the disease's heterogeneity.

papers.title: A genome-scale metabolic model of a pathosystem sheds light on bacterial wilt.
papers.abstract: During plant infection, complex metabolic interactions occur between the host and the pathogen, including direct competition for resources. While pathogens exploit host-derived nutrients to sustain growth and virulence, plants attempt to restrict pathogen proliferation by limiting nutrient availability. To quantify the contribution of these trophic interactions to disease development, we developed a mathematical model of plant-pathogen metabolism. A genome-scale metabolic model of the pathogen was integrated with a genome-scale, multi-organ metabolic model of the plant and calibrated using exp
papers.summary: This study presents a genome-scale metabolic model of a plant-pathogen system, integrating the metabolism of both the host and the pathogen to quantify how nutrient competition influences bacterial wilt disease. The model reveals that the pathogen exploits host-derived nutrients to sustain growth and virulence, while the plant attempts to restrict pathogen proliferation by limiting nutrient availability. This resource provides a framework for understanding trophic interactions during infection and could inform strategies for disease control.

papers.title: Genome-scale metabolic modelling identifies vaginal microbiome members as potential probiotics.
papers.abstract: Probiotic supplements are marketed for diverse health benefits, yet species inclusion often lacks functional rationale. Our survey of 352 over-the-counter probiotic products available in the USA revealed 36 unique microbial species. However, there is no clear link between species inclusion and the intended health benefit. Here, to address this gap, we developed HaPaPro, a collection of 1,012 genome-scale metabolic models spanning pathogenic, probiotic and host-associated bacteria, constructed from publicly available genome sequences. Flux balance analysis revealed that probiotic species fail t
papers.summary: This study developed HaPaPro, a collection of 1,012 genome-scale metabolic models from pathogenic, probiotic, and host-associated bacteria, to functionally evaluate probiotic species. Analysis of 352 over-the-counter probiotic products found no clear link between species inclusion and health benefits, and metabolic modeling identified vaginal microbiome members as potential probiotics with functional rationale.

papers.title: When the host is not healthy: Rethinking tuberculosis drug targets in comorbid conditions.
papers.abstract: Tuberculosis (TB) frequently coexists with chronic conditions such as diabetes, HIV, malnutrition, sarcoidosis, and lung cancer. These comorbidities profoundly reshape the host immune, metabolic, and tissue environments, disrupting pathways that govern Mycobacterium tuberculosis (Mtb) containment and altering responses to both antimicrobial and host-directed therapies. Yet, most TB drug discovery pipelines continue to rely on "healthy-host" experimental models, overlooking the biological heterogeneity of the populations most affected by the disease. In this review, we argue that TB should be r
papers.summary: This review argues that tuberculosis (TB) drug discovery pipelines are flawed because they rely on 'healthy-host' experimental models, ignoring the fact that TB frequently coexists with chronic conditions like diabetes, HIV, and malnutrition. These comorbidities reshape the host's immune and metabolic environment, altering how the bacteria respond to treatment. The authors call for rethinking drug targets to account for the biological heterogeneity of real-world patient populations.

papers.title: Integrated network toxicology and molecular docking revealed that 2-ethylhexyldiphenylphosphoric acid induces nephrotoxicity in chickens by activating the ROS/JNK pathway.
papers.abstract: 2-Ethylhexyl diphenyl phosphate (EHDPHP) is a widely used novel organophosphorus flame retardant. In recent years, EHDPHP has been absorbed into humans and animals through contaminated environments and food intake, causing substantial adverse effects. Therefore, the biological toxicity of EHDPHP has aroused widespread interest among researchers. However, research on the toxic damage mechanism of EHDPHP on the kidneys of poultry is still relatively limited. This study predicted the target sites of EHDPHP through network toxicology and molecular docking. Subsequently, chickens were administered
papers.summary: This study uses network toxicology and molecular docking to predict that the flame retardant EHDPHP causes kidney damage in chickens by activating the ROS/JNK pathway. The findings provide a mechanistic understanding of EHDPHP's nephrotoxicity in poultry.

papers.title: Nitric Oxide-Mediated S-Nitrosylation of TSC2 Drives mTOR dysregulation across Shank3 and Cntnap2 Models of Autism Spectrum Disorder.
papers.abstract: Autism spectrum disorder (ASD) is a complex neurodevelopmental disorder characterized by core behavioral symptoms. We previously showed that nitric oxide (NO) plays a key role in ASD. However, the precise molecular mechanism through which NO acts via its posttranslational modification, S-nitrosylation (SNO), in ASD remains largely unknown. Emerging evidence, including our previous studies, suggests that the mechanistic target of the rapamycin (mTOR) signaling pathway plays a critical role in ASD pathophysiology. Our SNO-proteome systems biology analysis showed the enrichment of the mTOR pathwa
papers.summary: This study identifies a new molecular mechanism in autism spectrum disorder (ASD) where nitric oxide modifies the TSC2 protein through S-nitrosylation, leading to dysregulation of the mTOR signaling pathway in two different mouse models of ASD (Shank3 and Cntnap2). The finding connects nitric oxide signaling to mTOR pathway dysfunction, offering a potential therapeutic target for ASD.

papers.title: Genome-scale metabolic models predict diet- and lifestyle-driven shifts of ecological interactions in the gut microbiome.
papers.abstract: Microbiomes and their host environments form complex, interconnected ecosystems. The microbial species within a microbiome, on the one hand, compete for resources, while on the other hand, they exchange vital metabolites to support their survival. These interactions are influenced by the microbial genetic repertoire, environmental conditions, and availability of nutrients. We developed EcoGS (http://www.github.com/KaletaLab/EcoGS), a metabolic modeling tool designed to predict the ecological interactions between pairs of microbes. Applying EcoGS to the microbiomes of two distinct human cohorts
papers.summary: This study introduces EcoGS, a new computational tool that uses genome-scale metabolic models to predict how pairs of gut microbes interact ecologically (e.g., competing or cooperating). By applying EcoGS to human gut microbiome data, the authors show that diet and lifestyle can shift these microbial interactions, offering insights into how environmental factors shape the gut ecosystem.

papers.title: Rhizospheric Microbes and Nanoparticles Synergize to Enhance Plant Immune Responses.
papers.abstract: Sustainable crop production increasingly requires innovative strategies that can enhance plant resilience while reducing dependence on synthetic agrochemicals. Recent advances in nanotechnology and rhizosphere microbiome research have created new opportunities to strengthen plant defense systems through integrated biological and material-based approaches. This review introduces the concept of nanobiotic synergies, defined as the strategic combination of engineered or biologically synthesized nanoparticles with beneficial rhizospheric microorganisms to improve plant immunity, stress tolerance,
papers.summary: This review proposes the concept of 'nanobiotic synergies,' where combining nanoparticles with beneficial soil microbes can boost plant immune responses and stress tolerance, offering a sustainable alternative to synthetic agrochemicals for crop production.

papers.title: Comparing metabolic engineering scenarios using simulated design-build-test-learn-cycles.
papers.abstract: Design-Build-Test-Learn (DBTL) cycles are a widely employed engineering framework in metabolic engineering. Nonetheless, their performance depends on a wide range of experimental and algorithmic design choices, whose combined effects on the successful optimization of microbial strains remain an open question.
papers.summary: This study uses computer simulations to compare different strategies for designing, building, testing, and learning (DBTL cycles) in metabolic engineering. The authors show that the choice of experimental and algorithmic parameters significantly impacts how efficiently microbial strains can be optimized. This work provides a framework for rationally selecting DBTL strategies to improve strain engineering outcomes.
